preFix.title: Painting a floor with floor paint
preFix.subtitle: Prepare and paint concrete, flagstone, or timber floors
preFix.excerpt: How to paint a floor: preparing a concrete or timber surface, applying a primer, rolling on two coats of floor paint, and giving the floor adequate cure time before use. Covers both concrete and timber floor paint systems.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus.

content.text: Painting a floor is one of the faster ways to transform a utility room, workshop, or garage. The preparation determines how long the paint holds: a floor paint applied to a dusty, contaminated, or damp surface will lift within weeks. A properly prepared surface lasts 5 to 10 years before it needs a fresh coat.

Tape a piece of polythene sheet (about 500 x 500 mm) to the floor with tape on all four edges and leave for 24 hours. If moisture condenses on the underside, the floor is damp and cannot be painted without addressing the damp source first. A damp-proof floor membrane or a damp-proof floor paint system is required, not a standard floor paint.

Sweep the floor thoroughly and scrub with a floor cleaner to remove oil, grease, and tyre marks. Grease is the most common cause of floor paint failure: scrub with a degreasing detergent, rinse, and allow to dry for at least 48 hours. Grind away any high spots and fill cracks and holes with a floor-repair compound. New concrete should cure for at least 28 days before painting.
and allow to dry fully. On a previously painted timber floor, sand lightly with 80-grit paper to provide a key, vacuum the dust, and wipe with a tack cloth before painting.

with a short-pile roller (6 to 9 mm nap). Cut in around the edges and obstacles with a brush first. Work from the far corner toward the exit door so you do not paint yourself into a corner. Apply in even passes, maintaining a wet edge across the full width. Allow the first coat to dry for the manufacturer's stated time (typically 4 to 6 hours for water-based floor paint) before applying the second coat.

Floor paint may be dry to the touch within hours but does not reach full hardness for several days. Allow 24 hours before light foot traffic, 2 to 3 days before moving furniture or heavy objects back in. Do not subject the floor to vehicle traffic for at least a week. In cool conditions (below 10 degrees Celsius), these times extend significantly.

attrs.fix: Strip back to bare concrete. Degrease thoroughly, check for and address damp, and re-prime before repainting.
attrs.cause: Grease or contamination on the surface, or the floor was damp at the time of painting.

attrs.fix: Light sanding with 180-grit paper will knock back roller texture before a further coat.
attrs.cause: Paint applied too thick, or second coat applied before the first coat was fully dry.
attrs.symptom: Roller marks visible in the dried finish
